justification: All pass criteria met: MD5 identified as cryptographically broken with specific attack vectors (collision, preimage, hashcat feasibility); HMAC-SHA256 explicitly recommended as replacement with correct code snippet; timing attack via string equality identified as critical with reproduction steps; crypto.timingSafeEqual recommended with Buffer conversion detail; timestamp design flaw thoroughly explained as broken API contract; safe_to_deploy false. Additionally covers length-extension attack risk, expiration enforcement gap, and JWT as alternative.

observations.text: MD5 finding includes concrete attack feasibility (10B hashes/sec on GPU, hours to crack) and cites NIST 2011 deprecation
Correctly identifies the hash-vs-HMAC distinction — notes secret concatenation is naive MAC construction vulnerable to length-extension attacks
Timing attack finding includes practical reproduction methodology and notes the 30-day window amplifies risk
Timestamp flaw correctly identified as broken API contract — not just inconvenience but tokens are unvalidatable
Production readiness section includes migration strategy (dual-validate old+new for 30 days)
Suggests JWT as standard token format solving multiple issues at once

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: MD5 identified as cryptographically broken and unsuitable for security tokens; HMAC-SHA256 explicitly recommended with warning against naive SHA-256 concatenation (length-extension); timing attack via string === identified with crypto.timingSafeEqual recommended; timestamp design flaw thoroughly explained (Date.now() discarded, validation impossible, no expiry enforced); safe_to_deploy false.

observations.text: Correctly identifies MD5 as broken with specific attack vectors (collision tools, GPU brute-force speed)
Explicitly recommends HMAC-SHA256 and warns against simply swapping to SHA-256 hash with concatenated secret due to length-extension attacks
Timing attack clearly explained with practical reproduction steps and correct fix (timingSafeEqual with length check)
Timestamp flaw thoroughly analyzed — notes both the impossibility of validation and the missing expiry enforcement
Goes beyond rubric by identifying the secret-concatenation length-extension vulnerability as a separate important issue
Identifies delimiter collision risk in partnerId input validation
Production readiness section includes practical migration/rollback strategy with token versioning

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: MD5 identified as cryptographically broken with specific attack vectors (collision, preimage, length-extension); HMAC-SHA256 recommended as the correct primitive with code example; timing attack via === identified with reproduction steps; crypto.timingSafeEqual recommended with length-check caveat; timestamp design flaw thoroughly explained (caller cannot know the timestamp, expiration unenforced); safe_to_deploy false.

observations.text: Correctly distinguishes hash vs HMAC — explicitly calls out that naive concatenation is vulnerable to length-extension attacks and that HMAC is the correct primitive for message authentication
Timing attack explanation includes practical reproduction methodology (varying one character, measuring latency distribution)
Timestamp finding goes beyond the rubric minimum — identifies both the return value gap AND the missing expiration enforcement
Provides actionable migration strategy with dual-validation during transition window
Suggests JWT as a standard alternative to rolling custom token schemes
